title: The Olmec ceremonial city of San Lorenzo flourishes
summary: On a raised plateau above the flood-prone rivers of southern Veracruz, in the humid Gulf coast lowlands of what is now Mexico, the earliest complex society in Mesoamerica reached its peak around 1200 BCE. Its inhabitants, whom modern scholars call the Olmec, moved dozens of colossal basalt boulders from mountains 60 kilometres away, carved them into three-metre-tall portrait heads of their rulers, and laid out a ceremonial city, San Lorenzo Tenochtitlan, that set the template for every later civilisation in the region, from the Maya to the Aztec.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Gulf coast of southern Mexico, where the states of Veracruz and Tabasco now meet, is one of the wettest and most fertile parts of North America. Rivers such as the Coatzacoalcos, the Tonala, and the Grijalva-Usumacinta wind through mangrove swamp and rainforest to the sea. The rich alluvium supported early experiments in maize agriculture from at least the fifth millennium BCE. By 1800 BCE villages of pole-and-thatch houses were dotted across the coastal plain, cultivating maize, beans, squash, and cotton, and fishing in the seasonally flooded lagoons.

Somewhere around 1500 BCE, on a high plateau of some 500 hectares standing about 50 metres above the surrounding floodplain between two branches of the Coatzacoalcos, one of these villages began to grow into something entirely new. The plateau itself, artificially reshaped and levelled by human labour over centuries, would come to be called San Lorenzo, after the modern hamlet at its edge; the archaeological name conventionally attached to it is San Lorenzo Tenochtitlan, though it has nothing to do with the far later Aztec capital of the same name.

San Lorenzo was rediscovered in 1938 by the American archaeologist Matthew Stirling of the Smithsonian Institution, who had already worked at the neighbouring Olmec site of Tres Zapotes. Full excavation began under Stirling from 1945 to 1946, was resumed and greatly extended by Michael Coe and Richard Diehl of Yale in 1966 to 1968, and has been carried out on a large scale since the 1990s by the Mexican archaeologist Ann Cyphers of the Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico. Their work has established that San Lorenzo was occupied from about 1750 BCE and flourished, at its peak of roughly 250 hectares of built-up plateau supporting perhaps 5,000 to 13,000 people, from about 1400 to 1150 BCE. It was the largest settlement in the Americas of its age.

image.caption: San Lorenzo Colossal Head, Monument 6, Museo Nacional de Antropologia, Mexico City.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The Olmec (a modern name derived from the Nahuatl word for 'rubber people', applied by the Aztecs a millennium and a half later to the descendants of the region) built at San Lorenzo the first true ceremonial architecture in Mesoamerica. The plateau's surface was terraced into elite residential compounds, plazas, and platform mounds. Elaborate drainage canals lined with basalt slabs, some of them reusing older sculpture, carried water off the plateau; the channels are among the earliest engineered infrastructure in the New World. Elite houses of pole and thatch were floored with red sand and coloured earths.

What has made San Lorenzo famous, however, are its stone monuments. Ten of the seventeen currently known Olmec colossal heads come from San Lorenzo alone. Each is a portrait bust, carved in the round from a single boulder of basalt, wearing a close-fitting helmet or cap sometimes interpreted as ballplayer gear. San Lorenzo Colossal Head 1, discovered by Stirling in 1945, stands 2.84 metres tall and weighs an estimated 25 to 28 metric tons; Colossal Head 5, still larger, is 3.4 metres tall and weighs closer to 40 tons. Their facial features (broad noses, thick lips, individualised expressions) have prompted a long and now largely defused debate about African contact, which serious Mesoamericanists reject; the current mainstream view treats them as portraits of specific Olmec rulers, each perhaps commissioned by the ruler himself and reworked by his successors.

The basalt itself came from the Cerro Cintepec, an extinct volcano in the Tuxtla Mountains some 60 kilometres to the northwest across seasonally flooded terrain. How Olmec engineers transported multi-ton boulders across that distance is one of the enduring puzzles of the site; the most plausible reconstruction involves rafts floated on the Coatzacoalcos and its tributaries during the summer flood, followed by log-roller haulage overland. Whatever the method, the effort must have been enormous, and it implies a political authority capable of mobilising labour on a scale unknown elsewhere in the Americas at the time.

Beyond the heads, San Lorenzo produced dozens of other monuments: throne-like altars carved with niched figures apparently emerging from cave mouths; standing were-jaguar figures combining human and feline features; stelae showing rulers in formal poses. Small-scale portable art in jadeite, serpentine, and other greenstones (much of it imported from Guerrero, hundreds of kilometres to the west) was produced in prodigious quantity. Ceramic figurines depict corpulent 'baby-face' figures whose ritual significance remains debated. Trade goods recovered from San Lorenzo include obsidian from at least six different Mesoamerican sources, jade from Guatemala, and iron-ore mirrors polished to concave perfection.

Around 1150 BCE, at what most excavators identify as the site's peak, a remarkable episode occurred. Many of the colossal heads and other monuments were deliberately defaced, mutilated, and buried in ridges outside the ceremonial precinct. Whether this represents an internal political revolution, a religious reformation, a change of dynasty in which each new ruler symbolically buried the images of his predecessors, or a foreign conquest is unresolved. The site continued to be occupied, but its central status passed within a few generations to La Venta, some 90 kilometres to the east in modern Tabasco, which flourished from about 900 to 400 BCE and produced its own colossal heads and its own extraordinary jade offerings.

image.caption: Olmec seated were-jaguar jade figurine, American Museum of Natural History, New York.
image.credit: Photograph by Madman2001, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The Olmec did not vanish after San Lorenzo's decline; the tradition simply migrated eastward to La Venta and, later, back westward to Tres Zapotes, before dissolving into successor cultures across the Gulf coast by around 400 BCE. But the cultural template established at San Lorenzo, monumental sculpture representing rulers, sacred precincts oriented on cardinal directions, an iconography combining human and animal features, ritual objects in imported greenstone, and long-distance trade networks reaching hundreds of kilometres, persisted and elaborated throughout Mesoamerican history.

Olmec influence radiated outward. Highland sites at Chalcatzingo in Morelos and Teopantecuanitlan in Guerrero show Olmec-style bas-reliefs cut into living rock, thousands of kilometres from the Gulf heartland. Small Olmec-style jade figurines have been recovered from Chiapas, from the Pacific coast of Guatemala and El Salvador (where the Mokaya culture had been experimenting with ceremonial architecture even before San Lorenzo's peak), and as far south as Costa Rica. The mainstream view treats the Olmec as the 'mother culture' of Mesoamerica in a genealogical sense, though a rival view, associated most prominently with the archaeologist David Grove, sees them as one of several roughly contemporary 'sister cultures' whose shared features emerged from long interaction rather than descent from a single source.

Specific Olmec innovations became Mesoamerican universals. The rubber ballgame, played on I-shaped courts with a solid rubber ball and hip-only contact, is attested from the earliest Olmec levels and continued in continuous use for three millennia; the Aztecs were still playing it when Cortes arrived in 1519 CE. The were-jaguar iconography evolved into the rain god Tlaloc of the highlands and into Maya Chaak. The bar-and-dot numerical system, ritual calendars, and possibly early writing (a series of Olmec-related symbols found at San Andres and on the Cascajal Block, if genuine, would be the earliest writing in the New World) all trace back to Olmec origins.

The abandonment of San Lorenzo itself has been variously explained. Ann Cyphers's palaeoenvironmental work has shown that the rivers around the plateau changed course during exactly this period, potentially depriving the site of its river-borne food supply and defensive moat. Others emphasise internal factors, elite over-reach, ritual crisis, or peer-polity competition with the ascendant La Venta. What is clear is that the closing centuries of the second millennium BCE were a period of profound reorganisation in the Olmec world, not of collapse.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Olmec remained essentially forgotten by history from the time of their late-period dissolution until the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, when Mexican amateurs and European travellers began to notice curious sculptures being turned up by peasants in Veracruz and Tabasco. The first colossal head to enter modern awareness was found at Tres Zapotes in 1862 by Jose Melgar y Serrano, who published it in 1869 with speculations about African origins that would continue to bedevil the field for a century. In 1929 the American archaeologist Marshall Saville coined the term 'Olmec' for this hitherto anonymous civilisation, and in the 1940s Stirling's excavations at Tres Zapotes and La Venta finally demonstrated by stratigraphy and radiocarbon that the culture predated the Maya by more than a thousand years, a claim resisted by earlier Mayanists who believed the Maya were the mother civilisation of Mesoamerica.

Today the colossal heads are national icons of Mexico. Several stand in the Parque-Museo La Venta in Villahermosa and the Museo de Antropologia in Xalapa; others have been displayed in Washington, Paris, and Beijing. Excavation continues under Ann Cyphers and her colleagues, who have been carrying out systematic mapping and probing of the San Lorenzo plateau since 1990, and who have recently focused on the water-management systems that made the site habitable at all.

The deepest legacy of San Lorenzo is that it demonstrated, for the New World, what a state-scale society could look like: an elite that mobilised thousands of workers, a religious system that projected authority through monumental art, a trade network stretching across half a continent, and an iconography of rulership that would remain legible to Mesoamerican eyes for the next two thousand five hundred years. Every ballcourt, every stone throne, every rain-god mask in the region is, in some sense, a descendant of the plateau above the Coatzacoalcos.

image.caption: Olmec sculptures at the Parque-Museo La Venta in Villahermosa, Tabasco.

worldContext.text: In north China the late Shang dynasty was in its middle period, ruling from the great city at Yin (modern Anyang). The reign of Wu Ding (c. 1250 to 1192 BCE) had marked the peak of Shang power; his consort Fu Hao, whose intact tomb would be discovered by Chinese archaeologists in 1976, commanded armies of many thousands. The cast-bronze ritual vessels of the age, produced by piece-mould technology unique to China, are among the great artistic achievements of the ancient world.

The eastern Mediterranean was in the throes of the Late Bronze Age collapse. Around 1180 BCE the Hittite empire fell and its capital Hattusa burned; Ugarit on the Syrian coast was destroyed in flame with its last cuneiform tablets still baking in the ovens; and in Egypt the pharaoh Ramesses III fought his great naval battle against the coalition of migrating Sea Peoples in the Nile delta, an engagement he commemorated in the vast reliefs of his mortuary temple at Medinet Habu near Thebes.

In the Aegean the Mycenaean palace civilisation was disintegrating. Pylos in the southwestern Peloponnese was destroyed by intense fire around 1180 BCE, its last Linear B tablets preserved by the flames that consumed the archive; Mycenae and Tiryns were repeatedly damaged. Farther north in Bronze Age central Europe the Urnfield culture, with its cremation rite and characteristic ceramic burial urns, was spreading across the middle Danube. In the British Isles the Deverel-Rimbury tradition of late Bronze Age settlement and field systems was flourishing.

Along the middle Nile, Egyptian control over Nubia was still nominally in place, but the Kushite provinces were becoming increasingly restive; within another century the Egyptian administration would withdraw and local kingdoms would re-emerge. Farther west and south, the Sahara had by this era completed its long desiccation from the wetter conditions of the mid-Holocene; pastoralist populations who had grazed cattle across a green Sahara several millennia earlier had retreated to the desert margins and to the Nile valley.

In the northwestern Indian subcontinent the Late Harappan phase was in its final centuries. The great planned cities of Mohenjo-daro and Harappa were essentially abandoned; smaller village societies filled the plains. Indo-Aryan speakers, whose ancestors had moved out of Central Asia in earlier generations, were consolidating in the Punjab. The earliest hymns of the Rigveda were probably being composed in this era, though the collection would not reach its final form for another millennium.

In the western Pacific the Lapita cultural complex, marked by its distinctive dentate-stamped pottery, was at its expansive peak. Austronesian voyagers in double-hulled outrigger canoes had spread from the Bismarck Archipelago through the Solomons to Vanuatu, New Caledonia, and Fiji; within another two centuries they would reach Tonga and Samoa, the first human beings to set foot on the islands of Remote Oceania. Australia continued to be inhabited by its long-established populations of Aboriginal peoples.

figures.name: Matthew Stirling
figures.role: American archaeologist of the Smithsonian Institution whose excavations at Tres Zapotes, La Venta, and San Lorenzo from the 1930s and 1940s established the Olmec as a distinct civilisation

figures.name: Michael D. Coe
figures.role: American archaeologist whose Yale excavations at San Lorenzo in 1966 to 1968 defined the site's chronology and cultural sequence

figures.name: Ann Cyphers
figures.role: Mexican archaeologist of the Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico who has led systematic excavations at San Lorenzo since 1990

figures.name: Richard Diehl
figures.role: American archaeologist and co-director of the Yale San Lorenzo project, later a leading synthesiser of Olmec studies

images.caption: San Lorenzo Colossal Head, Monument 6, one of ten such basalt portrait heads from the site, now in the Museo Nacional de Antropologia in Mexico City.
